highlights: The Ciompi Uprising When Florence’s lowest-paid textile workers seized power, and how betrayal and ambition brought their revolution to a  ...    Unrest had been fomenting in Florence, especially in the Santa Croce and Oltrarno neighbourhoods, before it broke out into what started, at least in the beginning, as a bloodless coup on June 24, 1378, which was led by the humblest of workers in the cloth industry, including day labourers, wool combers, dyers and those doing poorly paid jobs like the many shop boys employed in the trade. Known as the Ciompi, deriving from the French word compère (meaning “buddy” or  ...    The revolt was sparked by the election of Salvestro de’ Medici, a cousin of the banking branch of the family, who was nominated gonfalonier by popular acclaim on June 18, 1378. title: The Ciompi Revolt of 1378
highlights: Ciompi Revolt of 1378: Socio-political Constraints and Economic Demands of Workers in Renaissance Florence  ...    Despite extensive research on the Ciompi Revolt, historians have agreed upon little with regard to the causes, course, and after-effects of the revolt. For all intents and purposes, it was the result of class conflicts and other tensions that were ready to rupture and implode after decades of build-up. The seven major guilds controlled seventy-five percent of all government positions within Florence, the fourteen minor guilds only twenty-five percent. Meanwhile, the Ciompi (wool workers) and the other popolo minuto ("little people") powered the Florentine economy, yet as a group nevertheless remained the grossly underpaid subjects of the guilds. Thus they suffered with the vicissitudes of the  ...    months, examining the tensions within the city and looking for the long-term impact of this revolt.
